data.title: Coeur's Next Leg Is About Duration, Not Just Scale
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In a piece I did in February on Coeur Mining (CDE), I said that CDE's portfolio was beginning to act like a portfolio and not like a single mine kind of a bet. Well, as of writing this, I still believe that. Though, now I think that the story has moved one step further. And I am saying this because CDE is no longer just moving from build mode to harvest mode, as I mentioned in the previous article. It is moving into what I would like to call a duration mode. And by this, I mean that CDE is not just generating more cash flow. But it is also extending where the cash can come from while also beginning to return some of it to its shareholders. And that is why I am reiterating CDE as a Strong Buy.

First and foremost, CDE completed the New Gold acquisition on March 20. Then on March 23, the company came back and consolidated 2026 guidance. A much bigger capital return framework and also updated technical evidence that New Afton and Rainy River add more than simple scale. The company now expects 2026 consolidation production between 680k and 815k ounces of gold. Additionally, CDE also expects 18.7 to 21.9 Moz of silver and between 50 and 65 million pounds of copper. I also note that CDE authorized a $750 million repurchase program, launched an inaugural $0.02 semiannual dividend, and also added a $1 billion revolver. In my view, this shows that the management is now thinking like a company with options and not one still crawling out of a capital hole.

Secondly, I think the reserves story keeps getting better. When I wrote about Coeur back in Feb., the company said that proven and probable reserves rose to 4.4 million ounces of gold and 274.4 Moz of silver. Wharf was up by 65% in gold reserves. Palmarejo on the other side was up by 36%. And Kensington is up 9%. Then the March update added the maiden K-zone resource at New Afton and a 2-year mine life extension. And this is what I think the market is still not fully appreciating. This is because CDE's growth story is not only about finishing Rochester, integrating Las Chispas, or closing New Gold. I believe it is about using stronger cash flow to make the portfolio last longer and pay more back over time.

Business Overview

Coeur is now a 7-asset North American precious metals producer. After closing New Gold, the portfolio now includes New Afton and Rainy River in Canada, Las Chispas and Palmarejo in Mexico, and Rochester, Kensington, and Wharf in the US. There is also Silvertip in British Columbia as a longer-dated option. So yes, CDE still has a big silver exposure. But it now has a much more balanced gold, silver, and even copper mix. This makes the earnings stream cleaner than what many investors still assume when they think of the old CDE.

The CEO, Mitchell Krebs, put the strategic shift in a way I agree with when he said that "today marks an important milestone in Coeur's transformation to the sector's newest senior precious metals producer." To me, CDE is not the old mid-cap miner that was fighting for relevance. It is a real sector platform now. And I think that will change how investors will eventually compare it to peers.

Growth Drivers

I think the financial step-up in 2025 was already strong enough to support my thesis. For context, CDE reported 2025 revenue of $2.1 billion, a full adjusted EBITDA of $1 billion, and FCF of $666 million. The company also ended the year with net cash of about $554 million, and total debt decreased to $341 million. Now, what I find interesting is how cash generation is spreading across the portfolio. I say this because in the Q3 2025 internal earnings memo, the management talked about the "power of our 5-asset portfolio. " At that time, Las Chispas, Palmarejo, Rochester, Kensington, and Wharf were all contributing meaningful FCFs. Yet, CDE expected 2026 to be a record year while emphasizing flat input costs alongside rising metal prices. This is evidence that margin expansion is not only being handed to CDE by the metal tape. But rather, I would say there is real operational discipline underneath it.

In my opinion, the growth opportunity is not just that CDE is bigger. I think the opportunity is that CDE is starting to deserve better valuation because the future cash flow base is getting more durable. Wharf's mine life nearly doubled to 12 years. Palmarejo, on the other hand, added resource depth to the east, including areas outside the Franco-Nevada stream footprint. I also note that Kensington has kept building out its longer life blueprint. And New Afton now brings K-Zone optionality. As if that is not enough, Rainy River got 2 additional years of mine life. And Silvertip is still sitting there as a longer-term, silver-heavy option. Just in case Coeur chooses to accelerate it with a larger platform and deeper Canadian footprint.

And this is why the New Gold close matters to me more than the headline market cap. In the update, I note that the CEO said that the combined company could use its larger cash balance to invest in K-Zone, Rainy River exploration, Palmarejo, Las Chispas, Kensington, and Silvertip. All this while discussing higher capital returns after closing. In my view, when a miner talks after already reaching net cash and after already demonstrating strong FCFs, then it is something worth looking at in detail.

CDE is expected to release their Q1 2026 earnings results in May. In that report, I am looking to see how the market will use the latest update to test whether the post-close narrative is a real operating trajectory. I believe that if the Q1 2026 report supports the March story, then I believe that the stock can keep rerating higher.

Valuation

Valuation still looks interesting to me. Especially given that the stock still scores a D+ valuation grade.

Nonetheless, the first thing I notice is the mismatch between growth and FWD valuation. The growth screenshot below shows strength. Revenue growth, EBITDA growth, EBIT growth, and operating cash flow growth are all above sector medians. But when it comes to the valuation grades, CDE trades at a TTM non-GAAP P/E of 22.9x, while the sector median stands at 19.36x. I note that trailing valuation multiples are still lagging behind. But when I look at FWD multiples, the story changes. CDE trades at an FWD non-GAAP P/E of 9.29x, while the sector median trades at 16.44x. On the other side, the FWD EV/EBITDA of CDE stands at 5.79x, while the sector median stands at 8.58x, implying a 32% reduction.

On an FWD non-GAAP PEG basis, I note that CDE's number is missing. And that could be due to the fact that it's too small. Already with the TTM GAAP PEG, I can get an idea of where the FWD number would fall. But even without it, the message is clear.

When I compare to peers, I note that CDE is still at attractive levels. Newmont Corp. (NEM) has its FWD non-GAAP PEG at 0.44x. On the other side, Barrick Mining (B) has its FWD non-GAAP PEG ratio at 0.36x while Agnico Eagle (AEM) stands at 0.80x. So clearly, CDE still stands in a better position than even peers.

Key Risk

The biggest risk I see is that if gold and silver prices roll over before the market fully pays for CDE's improved duration, then the stock can still devalue quite fast. And this is mining. Metal prices still do a lot of talking. I also note that production costs, margins, and expected benefits from acquisitions all remain sensitive to market prices and operating execution. The second risk I see is integration. CDE closed New Gold only in March. So that means that the operating story is now bigger and better. But it is also more complex. CDE still has to integrate people, systems, reporting, and capital priorities while also preserving the momentum at New Afton and Rainy River. That said, I do think that CDE has some credibility here after integrating Las Chispas and after getting Rochester to the other side of its heavy investment cycle.

Final Thoughts

In my opinion, Coeur Mining has earned a more nuanced bullish case than the one that the market still seems to be using. The old version of the story was that Rochester was finally done, Las Chispas made the portfolio better, and New Gold could make CDE bigger. All of that still matters. But the stronger version is that CDE is becoming a company that can generate cash, extend mine life, and return capital at the same time. And that is the combination that I am vouching for. That is why I am reiterating Coeur with a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: ASGI: Potential To Capture Growth Of AI Data Centers
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI), I downgraded my rating to a hold due to the expensive valuation at the time. Since then, the fund has experienced positive growth momentum and delivered a double-digit total return that outpaces the S&P 500 Index over the same time frame. However, I believe that ASGI may have some renewed growth catalysts that can continue to take the share price higher over the next twelve months. The fund is directly aligned to benefit from the growing power demands from the rise of AI data centers. There are already several positions within ASGI that have demonstrated increased earnings capacity.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, ASGI's share price has increased by more than 31.3%. While the rest of the markets pulled back from their highs, ASGI was able to benefit from the market's rotation out of technology stocks. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up slightly above 48% over the same time frame. ASGI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.3% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. I believe that the potential for increased profitability can lead to better tax efficiency for the distributions going forward.

The tricky part is the fund's valuation at this time. Following the recent price rally, ASGI now trades at one of its highest price-to-NAV levels ever. So while I am optimistic about the outlook of the fund, finding an attractive entry can be a challenge. Furthermore, the outlook has the risk of being suppressed by the elevated interest rate environment. A high interest rate environment can disrupt growth and slow the rate of earnings growth for the fund.

Fund Strategy

Based on the latest fact sheet, ASGI has total net assets of $741.2M that are spread across a diverse range of infrastructure-related investments. The fund's primary objective is to provide a high total return while emphasizing income generation for investors. To achieve this goal, the fund doesn't utilize any sort of fancy option strategies or invest in any debt securities. Furthermore, the fund doesn't utilize any sort of leverage as part of its portfolio strategy. This makes the fund a more stable performer that should be able to avoid any wild price swings through periods of market uncertainty.

So to achieve its goal, ASGI simply invests in high-quality businesses that can appreciate in value or provide sustainable cash flows over time. This means that management is looking to identify high-quality companies that have some sort of moat within their respective industries while also prioritizing companies that have consistent cash flows and frameworks that allow for earnings growth over time.

What makes ASGI unique is that it also provides shareholders with exposure to private investments. For instance, ASGI's largest holding is currently labeled as 'Private Investment 9' at 4.4% of total assets. Within its top ten holdings, we can see that there are several private holdings that comprise a meaningful portion of its positions. This exposure to private companies can be a bit of a double-edged sword; on one end, this has the potential to expand earnings if the investments are successful. Conversely, this means investors have limited transparency into how these private investments are performing, and it is harder to keep tabs on their health, unlike public positions.

For instance, we can see there are public investments within ASGI's top ten holdings as well. This includes companies like Ferrovial SE (FER), Helios Towers plc (HTWSF), NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE), and International Container Terminal Services, Inc. (ICTEF), just to name a few. These are much easier to keep tabs on and for investors to assess their earnings over time.

We can see that the fund also leans most heavily on industrial investments at 28.2% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to utilities and energy investments, which account for 27.8% and 9.9% of assets, respectively. Therefore, investors need to expect that ASGI's daily price movement and total return potential are specifically linked to these focus areas since they make up nearly 66% of total assets. Furthermore, ASGI provides some global exposure to regions outside the US. 58.1% of total assets are still allocated to North American positions, but there is meaningful exposure to regions across Europe, Asia, and Africa.

Outlook: AI Data Centers

While industrials and utility companies have typically maintained a reputation for slow but steady earnings growth, I think this may change over the next few years. Although this remains true, I believe there are renewed growth catalysts related to the expansion of the AI data center market. While the majority of the market is focused on the software side of things, such as the LLMs like Gemini, ChatGPT, or Claude, there is a growing need in the background. This is where ASGI's positions are directly aligned to capitalize and grow earnings, which can lead to higher earnings growth for ASGI.

For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI data center market can increase to $810.6B by 2033. This would represent an estimated CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 23.9% from the current levels. Even if this data were overestimating the growth by a 50% margin, this could still indicate a double-digit growth rate across the size of the market over the next few years. After reviewing ASGI's holdings, I was able to locate several holdings that are already positioning themselves to see rapid growth from the rising demand.

  • NextEra Energy: management confirmed that the business expects to build 15-30 GW of new generation capacity for U.S. data centers by 2035.
  • Cheniere Energy, Inc. (LNG): Canada is looking to boost natural gas flows to the U.S. to help meet the power demands for AI data centers, which directly positions LNG to benefit. The business is seeing so much demand that they are already running at full capacity.
  • Ferrovial SE: The business is seeing a record pipeline of infrastructure projects in the U.S. The business recently acquired Powernet, which helps with strengthening its data center construction area.

Although a lot of the data support the idea that ASGI is positioned to capture this growth, it still comes down to whether this growth momentum can continue. If there's a sentiment shift in the outlook of AI data centers, this optimism can quickly fade into skepticism. We've already seen this happen over the last year; investors would rejoice if a company mentioned AI on its earnings call. There was a lot of optimism around AI, and now the market is a lot more pessimistic about AI. Between the massive capex spending levels, shrinking free cash flows, and uncertainty around future earnings growth, the market has punished software companies and is less excited about all the high spending from the large-cap leaders.

Valuation & Risks

At the time of my last coverage, ASGI traded at a premium to NAV of 3.67%. Following the recent rally in the first quarter of 2026, ASGI now trades at a slightly larger premium to NAV of 6.20%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that ASGI now trades near the highest premium to NAV level ever! For reference, ASGI has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.5% over the last five-year period. Therefore, valuation here is a bit tricky because, from this standpoint, it is an expensive time to accumulate shares.

However, there is nothing stopping ASGI from continuing to trade at a premium to NAV valuation going forward. A premium to NAV valuation can also be a direct reflection of the market's confidence in ASGI. So if the growth thesis is correct and the fund can substantially increase its NAV and earnings because of the growing power demand from AI data centers, it makes sense that the fund would continue to trade at a premium to NAV valuation. After all, it isn't unusual for high-quality closed-end funds to consistently trade at a premium.

The worry that I have is that there is also nothing stopping the fund from experiencing a selloff if growth momentum slows and isn't as rapid as the market expects. In this scenario, ASGI may be punished, and the share price may fall back into the discount-to-NAV territory. I believe that a big influence can be the future movement of interest rates. During the latest Fed meeting, it was decided that interest rates would be left unchanged.

Although the fund doesn't use any leverage, high interest rates can be a challenge for industrial, utility, and energy companies. A high cost of debt may negatively impact operating spreads for the positions within ASGI that do rely on debt in some capacity. This may cause businesses to push growth initiatives on their backlog, which can cause earnings to grow more slowly than anticipated. I also believe that if we ever face a scenario where interest rates rise, it will cause these investments to grow a bit slower.

Since the fund doesn't utilize any sort of debt securities or option writing to generate the amplified income needed to support distributions, the fund relies on the growth of its positions. This means that ASGI has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions, so its holdings aren't capable of growing over time without interruption. So when measuring ASGI's performance over time, this translates to limited capital appreciation compared to more traditional approaches like the State Street ETFs for the industrial sector (XLI) or the utilities sector (XLU).

Increased Tax Efficiency For Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.23 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.3%. Looking at the fund's payout history below, we can see that ASGI has maintained steady distributions and even raised payouts starting in 2024. This has made ASGI an excellent investment for income investors who want to prioritize a stream of supplemental income within their portfolio. Furthermore, the consistent payouts enable investors to see their annual dividend income grow, especially if investors choose to reinvest a portion of their distributions.

However, I wanted to specifically highlight the potential for tax efficiency going forward. If my growth thesis is correct, this means that ASGI will see a lot of its positions rise in value, and it will need to sell those positions to support distributions.

This means that ASGI could possibly prioritize supporting those distributions through long-term capital gains. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 69% of the distributions paid YTD were supported by net realized long-term capital gains, which are classified as qualified dividends and have favorable tax treatment for investors. As long as ASGI is capable of expanding on long-term capital gains, the overall tax liability can be minimal for investors who are utilizing ASGI outside traditional tax-advantaged accounts.

However, the possible boost in tax efficiency doesn't necessarily equate to tax-free. As we can see, there is still a portion of distributions that are being supported by the combination of net investment income and net realized short-term gains, which are both classified as ordinary dividends and have the highest tax burden for investors. Of course, these concerns can be offset if you hold ASGI within a tax-advantaged account, but it's worth mentioning for investors who typically use a regular brokerage account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that ASGI remains a hold for now due to the elevated price to NAV valuation. Although I am optimistic about the outlook of the fund, I would like to wait for some updated reporting to get some transparency into earnings and NAV growth. I would need confirmation that the fund's earnings justify the current premium and that it can be sustained going forward. If you are an income investor who is optimistic about the outlook of AI data centers, ASGI is a great way to position your portfolio for success. However, investors who are a bit skeptical about the future growth of AI data centers may benefit from waiting on the sidelines until a better entry opportunity appears.

data.title: CoStar: Even After The Fall, This Stock Remains Vulnerable In The AI Age
data.description:
Though the stock market has swiftly rallied back near all-time highs as investors enter the Q1 earnings season with hope for an Iran deal, many companies remain in bear market territory, particularly tech stocks that are considered to be at risk for the coming AI revolution. In my view, the selling has been indiscriminate, and I think AI will impact individual companies in different ways: the key now is for investors to determine the potential winners from the losers—and among the winners, we must be sure as well to enter into the stocks at a good price.

CoStar Group (CSGP), in my view, has neither the technology moat nor the valuation multiples to justify a buy. Despite a 40% drop in the stock this year, we find insufficient compelling evidence that the company is capable of achieving the significant growth acceleration that the stock is banking on to grow into its rich multiples.

I'm initiating CoStar at a sell rating. To me, while there are certainly bullish catalysts underneath this stock, there are equally concerning risks, not least of which is the stock's rich multiples. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

A data juggernaut for commercial and residential real estate

Let's first kick off the discussion with an overview of what CoStar does. Through a disciplined M&A strategy over the past decade, CoStar has built up one of the most wide-ranging data infrastructure platforms in the real estate industry.

The chart below showcases the company's enterprise/commercial offerings. CoStar, which is the company's flagship brand, is a data provider for real estate professionals, ranging from commercial agents to developers and major property owners. Its value-add lies in giving industry participants a view of transactions comparable, particularly in leasing data, which is far more opaque than property sales.

LoopNet is closely related to CoStar. Acquired by the company in 2012, think of LoopNet as a Zillow/Redfin but for commercial properties, such as office buildings, multifamily developments, hotels, and retail shopping centers. LoopNet offers properties both for sale and for lease.

Matterport, which is the company's most recent acquisition (bought in 2025 for $1.6 billion), is a 3D mapping service. An increasing number of real estate listings now use Matterport software to give viewers accurate 3D renders and virtual tours of indoor spaces. Meanwhile, BizBuySell is arguably the least related of CoStar's various subsidiaries, functioning as an online marketplace for small businesses for sale.

The snapshot below showcases the company's residential/consumer businesses:

Think of Homes.com and Apartments.com, for sale and lease respectively, as CoStar's answer to Zillow. These are large listing networks that are increasingly drawing in consumer traffic for those searching for their next home.

Management believes, according to third-party data from comScore, that these brands are achieving significant share gains vis-à-vis Zillow. Per CEO Andrew Florance's remarks on the recent Q4 earnings call:

According to comScore, visits to Apartments.com network were up 14% year-over-year in January. Our growth is in contrast with comScore's data that showed that Zillow's rental traffic was down 48% year-over-year, and Zillow's partner Redfin rentals, was down 46% year-over-year. Zillow's expanded rental network, Zillow, Realtor, Redfin, was down 29% year-over-year in January. Zillow's rental revenue fell sequentially in the fourth quarter compared to the third quarter as the rental traffic was falling.

With traffic falling, our closest competitor is now doing something called shotgunning leads. That's encouraging potential renters to contact not just the person you intend to contact but all the competitors' properties. And while this increases and distorts the number of pure leads, it significantly lowers the quality of the leads and therefore, hurts ROI and lead to lease conversion rate. Poor leads create more work for the apartment community as they sort through these leads to ultimately produce less leases."

Homes.com, in particular, has been the focus of significant investment from CoStar recently, and one of the reasons Third Point Capital initiated (and later dropped) a battle against the company's board, citing that CoStar was sinking too much into consumer businesses that it viewed to be a distraction from the core commercial businesses that have fewer competitors.

Over the past year, Homes.com has released Homes AI. The core feature of Homes AI is to leverage contextual search to help homebuyers search available inventory rather than scrolling through listings. It helps to answer questions about neighborhoods, schools, price, and inventory data. While I agree that this feature is a key distinguisher versus Zillow, I do think the intent may be misguided: many home shoppers don't know what they're looking for or what parameters they need in a home, and many enjoy scrolling through home listings anyway.

CoStar has framed 2024 and 2025 as major investment years for the company, particularly to build out its residential AI capabilities. It's now positioning 2026 as the beginning of a multi-year period of accelerating growth and operating leverage, with a goal to 10x adjusted EBITDA (versus 2024 EBITDA of $241 million) by 2030. Keep this figure in mind: as versus FY25 actual EBITDA of $442 million, this requires an EBITDA CAGR of 40% through the next five years.

2026+ growth plans may run into the AI hiccup

Let's now zoom more closely into the company's near-term growth plans. For FY26, the company is guiding to $3.78-$3.82 billion in revenue, representing 19% y/y growth.

By segment, the company is noting that residential revenue of $1.83-$1.85 billion will carry the company's growth at 25-26% y/y thanks to recent traction on Homes.com/Apartments.com, while commercial will see slower 9-11% y/y growth. Importantly, however, the commercial segment has the richer margin profile in the mid-30s, while residential is only planned at mid-single-digit adjusted EBITDA margins. Overall company adjusted EBITDA of $740-$800 million reflects 74% midpoint growth and a 20.3% midpoint margin, ahead of the company's required 2030 growth goal CAGR.

The question we need to dissect is: is this growth profile realistic? First off, we note that one of the company's core initiatives this year is to invest heavily in its sales force. The chart below showcases the company's plan to grow sales headcount across key products through 2028:

The list below, meanwhile, showcases the company's specific investment areas. Management is planning on expanding the core CoStar brand into new markets, including Australia and France. It's also planning on significantly expanding the direct sales presence for Matterport, which is aimed to have a sales team of over 150 people versus 22 today.

There are also product releases responsible for a chunk of the planned growth. In particular, in line with the recent surge in demand for data centers, the company is planning on releasing a new lease comparables offering to compare data center lease terms.

CoStar is also planning on driving its own internal AI tools to scrape rental contracts to develop a new, sleeker rent comparables benchmarking product that makes for cleaner comparisons among the more opaque terms of commercial real estate deals, like tenant improvement allowances (TI credits, when landlords give leaseholders a budget for adapting space for their own needs) and other concessions. Again, per CEO Florance's remarks on the recent Q4 earnings call:

As discussed on previous earnings calls, we're developing a rent benchmark product, which we expect to deliver in Q2. Built upon the industry's largest collection of lease deals, CoStar rent benchmark uses AI to extract starting rents, TI allowances, rent concessions, escalations and more from the actual legal leasing document. After abstraction, leases are anonymized and aggregated to deliver the industry's only net effective rent product, allowing the user to understand the true cost of occupancy.

Corporate occupiers, owners and brokers alike will reap tremendous value from being able to tap into the largest source of verified lease information to inform their leasing decisions and more effectively manage their real estate portfolios."

This is where we feel it's prudent to inject some healthy skepticism into CoStar's plans. It's not lost on us that the company is expecting strong 18-19% top-line growth, which reflects no deceleration at all from 19% growth in FY25 despite a tough macro backdrop, especially for office leasing, against the surge of recent large-scale layoffs that will certainly reduce the overall demand for office space (and thus, CoStar's products). Second, we note CoStar's adjusted EBITDA forecast calls for a sharp ~7 point improvement in margins to 20.3% relative to 13.6% in FY25. And yet its growth plan hinges heavily on added sales hiring, plus a greater revenue mix into the less profitable residential segment, which will grow to nearly half of CoStar Group's revenue in FY26 at just a 5-7% margin.

Third, there's the AI factor to consider. Second-order macro impacts are clear here: less corporate headcount means less square footage needed for offices. And we should consider the direct competitive pressure as well. CoStar has long emphasized the proprietary nature of its data, leveraging aerial surveys to map land parameters and working directly with commercial brokers to learn the terms of new leases.

And yet, CoStar is just now touting a product that uses AI to glean lease terms. There's a chance that competing, nimbler AI companies that have more advanced R&D can break into CoStar's proprietary data advantage and make a similar comparables product that is much cheaper than CoStar, even if it's not 100% as complete as CoStar.

With all of these headwinds and realities stacked against it, we have to ask: is the company's 2026 outlook realistic, let alone its goal of growing to >$2 billion in EBITDA by 2030?

Valuation and key takeaways

Even if we take CoStar's outlook at face value and assume that the company can meet lofty expectations, it's also hard to ignore the fact that a good deal of strength is already priced into the stock—even after this year's spectacular correction.

At current share prices near $40, CoStar trades at a market cap of $16.98 billion. Netting off the $1.63 billion of cash and $993 million of debt on CoStar's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $16.34 billion.

Against the $3.80 billion and $770 million midpoints of the company's current revenue and EBITDA guidance, the stock trades at:

  • 4.3x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 21.2x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

Within real estate tech specifically, we prefer names like AppFolio (APPF), whose software embedded into large commercial multifamily operators makes it unlikely to be replaced by AI without causing major resident disruption, and Zillow (Z), whose consumer/residential franchises are better known than CoStar. And even against other non-real estate software companies that are similar growth peers to CoStar at a mid/high teens growth rate, like Atlassian (TEAM), the stock trades at a premium:

In short, we think CoStar's correction this year is an appropriate re-rating of its multiples, not a temporary panic-driven dip. Avoid this stock and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Halved Distribution Growth And Limited Iran Tailwinds Challenge Upside (Downgrade)
data.description:
I've covered Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) before, where I outlined the company's background in detail and explained why I considered it an attractive buy. Since then, the stock has returned more than 20%; however, at current levels I find the potential for further price appreciation quite limited given the expected 2026 growth. This is the main reason I am downgrading it to hold even though the distribution remains attractive at a 6% yield.

Background

Enterprise Products Partners is one of the largest midstream operators in North America. The partnership operates an integrated system across gathering, processing, fractionation, storage, and export infrastructure, and it remains a key part of the US energy value chain.

The business is mostly focused on natural gas liquids, with a 55% weight, with some diversification with crude and refined products accounting for almost 30% of the business and natural gas with a 16% weight. This gives Enterprise exposure across the different segments while remaining concentrated on the NGL.

Another characteristic is that around 80% of its gross operating margin is fee-based, roughly 15% is differential-based, and only about 5% is directly commodity linked. That is very relevant today, because it means Enterprise benefits much less from higher commodity prices than the recent move in the stock suggests. Since the beginning of the year, the stock has surged by 14%, while the benefits from the Iran war will be limited.

Despite this, the company continues to deliver steady distribution growth. Enterprise recently raised its quarterly distribution to $0.55 per unit, extending its long record of annual distribution growth. However, the increase was only a $0.005 increase (0.9%), while I was expecting a $0.01 increase, in line with previous years. I consider this deceleration a clear negative, and it makes justifying the current valuation more difficult.

On the operational front, Enterprise sold a 40% interest in the Bahia NGL pipeline to ExxonMobil for about $650 million and will connect the pipeline to Exxon Mobil's Cowboy gas processing plant. This is an interesting move as it monetizes part of the asset, adds a strong partner, and supports future growth; however, much of the upside will come after 2026.

Regarding CapEx, the company confirmed its previous indications about CapEx reductions, going from around $4.4 billion in growth capital during 2025 to between $1.9 billion and $2.3 billion in 2026. The 2027 CapEx is also expected to be around 2026 levels. This should help free cash flow; however, it will also reduce future growth.

Finally, management also increased the repurchase authorization by $3 billion, bringing the total program to $5 billion. I, personally, would prefer a higher distribution increase than repurchases at current levels; however, it provides management with optionality, especially since the near-term outlook also remains muted. During the conference call, management indicated that EBITDA and cash flow growth will be modest in 2026, probably around 3%, suggesting 2026 will be a transition year.

We're sort of looking at modest cash flow and EBITDA growth in 2026 compared to 2025. So probably at the lower end of that 3% to 5% range.

Financial Position and Valuation

Enterprise Products Partners has a strong financial position with approximately $1.2 billion in cash and about $34.7 billion of debt outstanding. Most of this debt is on very long maturities with an average maturity of almost 17 years; moreover, 98% is at a fixed rate, which provides clear visibility on interest expenses for many years.

The company also refinanced its short-term maturities due in 2026, issuing $1.65 billion of senior notes across different maturities at very attractive rates between 4.3% and 5.2%. The proceeds are being used to refinance debt coming due in early 2026.

The company reported Q4 earnings beating estimates at $0.75 EPS. For Q1 2026, I expect similar numbers with an EPS between $0.7 and $0.75. From Q2 I expect a slight improvement but nothing outstanding.

This makes me consider that the company is fully valued.

Enterprise units have moved higher with the broader midstream sector. However, the company will barely benefit, with only about 5% of its operating margin linked to commodity prices, as Enterprise will not materially benefit from higher oil prices. Most of its earnings are driven by NGL volumes, which are mostly maxed out, limiting the benefit from short-term commodity moves.

However, the company is trading above its historical averages from both a valuation standpoint (EV/EBITDA) and yield. While I consider the current valuation to be correct (12x EV/EBITDA) and the yield attractive, I see limited price appreciation potential. Moreover, with the distribution growth halved, I can't see yield moving much lower. These are the reasons I downgrade the stock to a hold.

Assuming EBITDA grows 5% in 2026, which is the high end of guidance, adjusted EBITDA would reach approximately $10.4 billion. Applying a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple would imply an enterprise value of around $104 billion, still below the current level near $115 billion. What remains attractive is mainly the yield, as Enterprise has one of the highest yields among larger midstream operators.

Risks

The main risk is that if the Iran war is resolved, it could move the stock lower, as evidenced by the stock's 10% slide since the tension de-escalated a few days ago.

Another potential risk could be its concentration in NGL, which provides more than 50% of the business; however, I personally like this concentration.

Finally, Enterprise carries a significant amount of debt and needs to refinance periodically. While the balance sheet is strong, a sustained increase in interest rates could pressure returns when the company refinances its maturities.

Conclusion

Enterprise Products Partners remains a high quality midstream operator with stable fee-based cash flows, a strong balance sheet, and a long track record of distribution growth.

However, after the company halved its distribution increase and following the recent stock rally, the valuation now looks stretched compared to its historical valuations. With growth expected at the lower end of the 3% to 5% range and only around 5% of earnings exposed to commodity prices, the current premium multiple seems difficult to justify.

At these levels, I can't see meaningful upside in the near term, and this justifies my downgrade to hold. The yield remains attractive but won't be enough to justify buying at the current price.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Cook Hands Apple To Ternus
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Apple (AAPL) CEO Cook steps down, effective Sept. 1; Ternus named CEO. (00:14) JetBlue's (JBLU) CEO dismisses bankruptcy speculation. (01:37) Uber (UBER) discloses 11.52% stake in Lucid (LCID). (02:35)

This is an abridged transcript.

After 15 years leading Apple (AAPL), Tim Cook is stepping down as CEO and will become Executive Chairman, effective September 1. He will be replaced by John Ternus, who had been Apple’s senior vice president of hardware engineering.

Apple shares fell 0.6% in premarket action following the announcement.

Cook will remain in his role as CEO through the summer and work with Ternus during the transition. Cook will still assist with certain aspects for Apple, including engaging with policymakers around the world.

Cook officially became Apple's CEO on Aug. 24, 2011, succeeding company co-founder Steve Jobs, who stepped down for health issues. Jobs passed away soon after, on Oct. 5.

“It has been the greatest privilege of my life to be the CEO of Apple (AAPL) and to have been trusted to lead such an extraordinary company,” Cook said in a statement. He continued: “John Ternus has the mind of an engineer, the soul of an innovator, and the heart to lead with integrity and with honor. He is a visionary whose contributions to Apple over 25 years are already too numerous to count, and he is without question the right person to lead Apple into the future.

Cook also shared a letter on Apple's website, expressing his thanks to the Apple community.

JetBlue Airways (JBLU) CEO Joanna Geraghty reassured employees in an internal memo that the carrier is not considering filing for bankruptcy protection this year. The comments were in reaction to speculation that the high cost of jet fuel would pressure the company's liquidity.

Geraghty noted that JetBlue (JBLU) has sufficient access to additional capital, including a recent $500M loan backed by aircraft, with an option to raise another $250M.

The speculation about JetBlue's (JBLU) financial position ramped up after founder David Neeleman publicly warned that if jet fuel prices average about $4.50 per gallon, JetBlue (JBLU) could lose around $1.3B in 2026.

Shares of JetBlue (JBLU) are up 28% on a year-to-date basis, indicating confidence from many investors. However, short interest on JetBlue (JBLU) stands at 18% of the total float.

Uber Technologies (UBER) disclosed an 11.52% passive stake in Lucid (LCID), representing over 37.75M shares.

The shares are held through SMB Holding, an indirect, wholly owned subsidiary of Uber, according to a regulatory filing.

Shares of Lucid are up 1.7% in premarket action.

Earlier on April 14, Lucid priced a $300M public offering of common stock, part of a larger $1.05B capital raise that includes investments from Uber and Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund.

As part of the pact, Uber (UBER) said it will increase its purchase commitment to at least 35,000 Lucid vehicles designed exclusively for use as part of Uber's future global robotaxi service. Uber has also committed to an additional $200M investment in Lucid, raising Uber's total investments to $500M to date.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

SpaceX said to host three days of analyst meetings at Starbase, Colossus this week

'Big Short' investor Burry says sudden crash after record surge would be uncharted territory

PG&E, Tesla approve Cybertruck and charging systems for residential V2X program in California

Catalyst watch:

  • GE Aerospace (GE) will hold its earnings call. Updates on GE's commercial engines/services growth, engine deliveries, supply chain execution, margin outlook, and guidance are anticipated. Options trading implies a 7% share price move after the release. Howmet Aerospace (HWM) is the stock with the tightest trading correlation to GE on earnings day.

Stock index futures are higher as renewed hopes for a peace deal between Iran and the U.S. lifted sentiment.

Crude oil is down 0.9% at $86. Bitcoin is up 0.5% at $76,000. Gold is down 0.7% at $4,784.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.1% and the DAX is up 0.6%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Amazon.com (AMZN) +3% - Shares gained after announcing a deeper AI push with Anthropic, committing an additional $5B investment with plans to scale up to $20B over time tied to milestones.

Economic calendar:

data.title: The More Things Change, The More They Remain The Same
data.description:
The first quarter of 2026 was so volatile that reviewing headline index performance sheds little light on economic fundamentals or market leadership. U.S. public equities declined by 3.9%[1]. Developed market (DM) equities fared little better, shrinking by a more modest 0.8%[2], underperforming their emerging markets (EM) brethren, which were flat[3]. Small caps outperformed large caps[4], and global fixed income slumped[5].

Heading into 2026, we expected the continued focus on gaining geostrategic autonomy - fueled by the proliferation of AI technologies - to power equity markets around the world. Our central thesis of “the revenge of the tangibles” focuses on next-generation defense, the electric tech stack, robotics, autonomous transport, and AI infrastructure buildout to drive economic growth and investment returns.

Investing in “the revenge of the tangibles” delivered outsized gains in the first quarter. Companies that derive at least 20% of their revenue from the abovementioned end-markets yield a universe of just under 2,000 names, which outperformed the broad MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI)[6] by more than 10% in the opening months of this year.

Relative Q1 Performance: Revenge of the Tangibles vs. MSCI ACWI IMI

Global Strength Meets AI Repricing

Global equities posted double-digit returns in the first two months of the year, while U.S. markets were busy digesting the implications of the unfolding AI rollout on existing software businesses.

For a decade, “capital-light good, capital-heavy bad” has been close to gospel: zero marginal cost, network effects, scaling laws, and high incremental margins. Some of this remains true, with new intangible models coming into view.

Yet the transition inevitably involves outright destruction, as many legacy software offerings were clunky, offering partial, often difficult-to-implement solutions. If AI removes some of these frictions, the associated products may no longer need to exist, or their price will be reduced to their marginal value.

In other words, AI has escaped the lab and is diffusing. We believe software companies must adopt and adapt and do so quickly and decisively.

While we expect a select group of incumbent software businesses may ultimately benefit from an expansion of their total addressable market (by leveraging their scale and competitive advantages to integrate AI and fight back against AI-native upstarts), nearly two decades of abundant capital and lofty valuations have fueled complacency and turned tailwinds into a sedative for many incumbents.

Furthermore, some companies are buying back stock with shareholders’ money, some are building “agents” to help with complexity within existing offerings, and many are sitting on the sidelines. A recent sell-side software analyst summed up the attitude of many software executives when he said, “This isn’t fair; it didn’t used to be like this.”

A Parallel from the Newspaper Era

While watching the current debate on software, we are reminded of the early 2000s debate over newspapers. Then, as now, stock prices moved well ahead of earnings downgrades: consensus forward estimates remained basically flat between 2002 and 2007, even as stock prices declined by 60%. But by 2011, earnings forecasts converged on stock prices, and both settled at about 10% of the 2002 average.

Management rhetoric then followed a familiar script.

  • Reframe as cyclical, not structural: During the advertising slump in 2007 and 2008, Gary Pruitt, then-CEO of newspaper publisher The McClatchy Company, said that a “significant portion” of the current troubles the industry faced were “cyclical.” The company went on to file Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2020.
  • Claim “hard-to-replicate moat”: Mike Reed, the CEO of GateHouse Media, which filed Chapter 11 in 2013, said in the company’s first quarter 2009 earnings release, “We view our exclusive local content as our greatest asset and as our sustainable competitive advantage.”
  • Argue scale will fix it: Dennis J. FitzSimons, who was the CEO of Tribune Company in 2003, said at the time, “Tribune will be bigger and in the top tier of its core broadcasting and newspaper businesses 24 months from now, as scale will be increasingly important in a fragmented marketplace.” The company filed for Chapter 11 in December 2008.
  • Emphasize current returns: Craig Dubow, who led Gannett Company before and during a restructuring between 2008 and 2010, said at the time the company’s core publishing business continued to generate significant cash flow even as print revenues declined. And Aaron Kushner, who became CEO of Freedom Communications after it filed for Chapter 11 in 2009, said that “We were profitable last year. … We will be profitable this year, profitable next year and profitable the following year.”
  • Move from denial to anger to acceptance: Real-estate investor Sam Zell was quoted saying, “I’m sick and tired of listening to everyone talk about and commiserate over the end of newspapers. They ain’t ended and they’re not going to end. I think they have a great future,” after completing the $8.2 billion leveraged Tribune Company buyout. Soon after Tribune’s bankruptcy, however, Zell offered a mea culpa during an April 2009 Bloomberg Television interview. He said, “The definition if you bought something and it’s now worth a great deal less, is you made a mistake. And I’m more than willing to say that I made a mistake. I was too optimistic in terms of the newspaper’s ability to preserve its position.” Zell added that the newspaper model in its current form did not work and needed to be acknowledged as such.

The Impact of the Iran War

And then came the Iran war. Oil prices shot up 60% to 70% between the start of the conflict through March 31, 2026, depending on the exact grade of crude. The U.S. dollar firmed slightly, and U.S. equities outperformed their DM and EM brethren. The U.S. economy is an oil and gas exporter and as such, is relatively insulated from the negative impact of scarce energy supplies.

The Iran war also interrupted the debate on software: between November 2025 and February 2026, software underperformed the S&P 500 Index[7] by 30%, but in March 2026, it performed in line with the broad index.

The latest military conflict in the Middle East has reinforced the case for accelerated defense spending by all those who can afford it, not to mention the rebuilding of U.S. military stockpiles.

And just as with prior episodes of high fossil energy prices, this one will accelerate the transition away from oil and gas. The more things change…

Footnotes

[1] The MSCI USA IMI is a broad equity index designed to represent the performance of the entire U.S. stock market across all size segments.

[2] The MSCI World ex USA IMI is a broad global equity index that measures the performance of DMs outside the United States.

[3] The MSCI Emerging Markets IMI is a broad equity index that tracks the performance of EM stocks across all company sizes.

[4] The MSCI ACWI Small Cap Index measures the performance of small-cap stocks across both DMs and EMs worldwide.

[5] The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index is a widely used benchmark for global investment-grade fixed-income markets.

[6] The MSCI ACWI is a broad global stock market index that measures the performance of publicly traded equities across the world.

[7] The S&P 500 Index is a stock market index that tracks the performance of 500 of the largest publicly traded companies in the United States.

Original Post

data.title: Commodity Price Watch: April 2026
data.description:
What is the overall trend in commodity prices?

The Middle East war continued to squeeze global oil and natural gas markets into the first half of April as the number of vessels traveling through the Strait of Hormuz remains close to zero. Our forecast assumes at least a partial recovery in oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz by the end of April, with a gradual ramp-up over May.

The major impacts from the Middle East war are on crude oil and refined products, natural gas, chemicals and plastics, and a few nonferrous metals. The Materials Price Index (MPI) by S&P Global Market Intelligence will peak in the second quarter, about 25% higher than the pre-war forecast. As measured by the MPI, industrial materials prices increased more than 10% in the first quarter of 2026 and are forecast for another double-digit increase in the second quarter. The process to pass through higher manufacturing costs will be limited by weaker demand, however.

The impact of high gasoline and other affected consumer goods prices leads to higher inflation that squeezes household budgets. A major impact will be lower near-term spending on big purchases like cars, appliances, and new homes, weighing on demand for industrial materials. The lower economic growth projections for major economies likewise imply a less upbeat outlook for businesses. At the time of writing, we are already about six weeks into the eight weeks this outlook assumes that trade through the Strait of Hormuz is near zero. The risk of a longer-lasting disruption, and thus tighter material supply and larger downward impact on demand, is significant and growing.

What is our forecast for energy-related commodity prices?

Global oil, refined product, and natural gas prices have rocketed higher and will remain elevated compared with previous forecasts through the end of 2026. Even after traffic returns to normal levels through the Strait of Hormuz, the pivot to build up inventories, coupled with the damage to production and export infrastructure, will leave fundamental supply tighter for several months.

At the time of writing, there is a ceasefire. The results of the impending negotiations are unknown; however, the ceasefire does imply a level of willingness to negotiate by all parties. If a more lasting truce can be achieved in this round of negotiations, this would fall in line with our assumptions.

The scale of disruption, however, cannot be fixed overnight. Damage to processing facilities and the restart of shuttered production will take time, leaving lasting damage to supply, the worst of which should be resolved in about six months. The negotiations are by no means guaranteed to head toward a truce, though. The longer traffic does not pass the Strait of Hormuz, the higher prices will go.

What is our forecast for chemical prices?

The chemicals and resins forecast was revised significantly upward across the board with our latest forecast. Higher pricing from upstream energy markets is partly to blame, but the overall push higher is being led by the general disruption to global supply chains coming from the war in the Middle East. Price rises across petrochemicals in the first half of 2026 will be broad, mirroring crude oil and natural gas increases.

The North American polyethylene forecast was revised significantly upward in this update. Higher pricing from upstream ethylene is partly to blame, but the overall push higher is being led by the general disruption to global supply chains coming from the war in the Middle East. What the significant global disruptions have done is reinforce the US position as a critical supplier to buyers in Europe, Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Risk to the forecast leaning toward the upside until more clarity is reached on the conflict in the Middle East.

The polyethylene forecasts for Europe and Asia also were revised significantly upward in this update.

What is our forecast for steel prices?

The impact of war has been more fully incorporated in April compared with March as more information can be considered. Prices were revised up for the US in the March Commodity Price Watch, and even more in April. US mills are pushing through price increases even though scrap is flat to down. Europe, however, has seen minimal revision since March as the trends are moving as expected.

Asia saw more of an initial jump than expected — about 5% — which is incorporated into the April Commodity Price Watch. However, little additional increases are sticking, so the March Commodity Price Watch profile of flat for 2026 rather than the pre-war uptrend is not changed.

Supply disruption is unlikely but may occur if war-related shipping issues arise or if temporary energy shortages curtail production. Demand will be flat to negative in Europe, the United States, and mainland China. Demand was already forecast to decline slightly, and the war will undercut even more.

What is our forecast for nonferrous metal prices?

Prices for aluminum have been revised up because of conflict-related higher energy prices and damage to facilities in the Gulf region. Copper prices have been revised down because of lower-than-expected increases from the Middle East conflict.

Prices for aluminum will remain elevated through 2026 on a combination of higher energy costs and reduced supply — both consequences of the Middle East conflict. At the same time, the conflict is hurting global economic growth, which will reduce demand for aluminum. Today, even with a two-week ceasefire in place, transport through the Strait of Hormuz has been significantly curtailed, and energy prices have continued to rise to hit new highs. This points to support for prices through the second quarter of 2026, even if there is an imminent, comprehensive resolution to the conflict.

Copper prices will ease over 2026 as supply concerns diminish, demand weakens, and financial speculation steps back to historic levels. As expected, the ongoing Middle East conflict has not brought as much upward price pressure, but disrupted trade and higher energy costs will support prices over 2026.

This article was published by S&P Global Market Intelligence and not by S&P Global Ratings, which is a separately managed division of S&P Global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BTX: A Strategy Reset, A 17% Discount, And Retail Access To Frontier AI
data.description:
The BlackRock Technology and Private Equity Term Trust (BTX) is a non-diversified closed-end fund (CEF) with a limited term (contingent perpetual) structure that invests at least 80% of its total assets in equity securities of U.S. and non-U.S. technology companies and privately held companies

I am initiating BTX on Seeking Alpha as a Buy. The setup found in BTX is the combination I look for in a CEF. A wide and widening price/NAV discount, a strategy reset that is now visibly producing, and a portfolio that is decisively positioned for the macro cycle the market is actually in. The rebuilt top 10 is a concentrated AI infrastructure book that pairs NVIDIA and optical/thermal/substrate beneficiaries with direct private exposure to all four of the most important frontier AI labs: Anthropic, OpenAI, PsiQuantum, and Deepgram, plus sub-top 10 exposure to Databricks. The fund has outgrown its BIGZ legacy on performance, and the discount has widened even as the underlying NAV has expanded. That is an interesting mismatch that I want to own.

BTX Now Vs. BIGZ Then

On January 21, 2025, BlackRock announced that BIGZ (BlackRock Innovation and Growth Term Trust) would be renamed BTX with a new investment strategy, new portfolio managers, and a new mandate focused on technology and private equity. The changes went into effect on February 20, 2025.

These are two funds stacked on top of each other. The first is BIGZ, which IPO'd in March 2021 at $20 per share into the peak of the innovation-growth bubble and proceeded to produce a -41.1% total return at NAV in 2022. The second is BTX post-February 2025, run by Tony Kim (who also runs BlackRock's flagship tech strategies, including BST and BSTZ).

The historical performance headline is brutal to look at. Looking at the BTX Q4 2025 commentary, the since-inception annualized total return at NAV is -10.2% against the MSCI ACWI SMID Growth/Information Technology Index at +8.1%, an 18% annual underperformance gap over the fund's entire life. After the strategy change, performance is narrower, but still negative, on a relative basis, with a 1Y NAV return of 0.8% versus the benchmark at 15.7%. Q4 2025 was the first genuine bright spot, with BTX delivering +1.5% at NAV versus -0.1% for the benchmark. Kim's turnaround has started showing, and that progress so far is promising. However, it is only one quarter, which isn't enough time to produce meaningful data.

This is the context an investor needs before the 8.51% distribution rate and the private AI holdings start to look attractive. BTX did not become a compelling vehicle by having a superb prior year. It became an interesting vehicle because the portfolio was torn down and rebuilt around a thesis that is now running into a favorable market.

Inside the ETF

Below are the Top 10 Holdings of BTX (using the BlackRock Fact Sheet from 03/31/2026):

The headline takeaway is that BTX now holds both Anthropic (3.52%) and OpenAI (2.59%) in the top 10, alongside PsiQuantum (7.10%) and Deepgram (3.51%). That is 16.72% of the portfolio in private frontier AI and quantum exposure, in a listed retail-accessible wrapper. To my knowledge, no ETF or mutual fund currently delivers this. BSTZ is the closest sibling, and BTX is the only vehicle that I am aware of that pairs OpenAI and Anthropic exposure in the same top 10. That alone is a structural reason to pay attention to the fund at current prices.

The Q1 2026 rotation away from the 12/2025 positioning gives a better overview of the strategy reset. PsiQuantum is now the single largest holding; NVIDIA was trimmed from the #1 position (8.45% at Dec. 31) down to #2 (6.40%); Tower Semiconductor stepped up materially to 4.06% from 2.85%; Lumentum moved up from 3.98% to 5.81%, and Kim and Menge added two new top-10 positions in OpenAI (private) and Asia Vital Components (Taiwan thermal/cooling). Celestica and Credo dropped out of the top 10 entirely. Trimming NVDA is also something to discuss. With NVDA appreciating materially in Q1, holding the dollar value roughly constant would have required trimming the share count, which is an active concentration-management decision rather than a signal of lost conviction. From what it looks like, the weight rotated into names further down the AI infrastructure stack (such as optical, thermal, substrate, and specialty silicon) and into the private labs that capture the demand-side economics as well.

Macroeconomic Discussion

The combination of Tower Semi, Lumentum, Fabrinet, Asia Vital Components, and Elite Material Co at nearly 17% of the portfolio is a high-concentration position on the parts of the AI buildout that have pricing power, with advanced packaging, optical transceivers, thermal management, and substrate materials being included. These are the bottlenecks in the supply chain, instead of the commoditized parts. Additionally, Asia Vital Components specifically (a Taiwan-listed thermal/cooling specialist) is a neat addition, considering the shift to liquid cooling and higher power densities in Blackwell and Rubin-class AI systems.

Additionally, the macroeconomic backdrop supports a concentrated bet within these themes. Worldwide data center capex rose 43% YoY in Q2 2025, with accelerated server spending up 76% on the ramp of NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra platforms across U.S. hyperscalers, neocloud providers, and sovereign AI projects. Additionally, it is further estimated that approx. 75% of aggregate hyperscaler capex in 2026 will fund AI-related infrastructure, or roughly $450 billion of AI-specific spending. This ETF has been positioned into names that can make the most money per dollar of capex from these investments. Lumentum will profit from optical interconnection, Fabrinet will profit from packaging, Tower Semiconductor will profit from specialty silicon, Asia Vital Components will profit from thermal, and Elite Material will profit from PCB substrates.

Private Equity

BTX held 21 private companies listed within their Q4 commentary for a combined valuation of ~$291 million (32% of the portfolio) at YE 2025. Private equity has increased since then, with the Q1 2026 fact sheet revealing a new position in OpenAI (2.59%) and step-ups to allocation in Anthropic (3.52%, up from 2.69%) and Deepgram (3.51%, up from 3.42%). There are a few things to discuss with the funds PE exposure.

First, the PE positions are held as preferred stock, not common equity. Preferred stock in a private equity context carries a liquidation preference that sits ahead of common equity in an exit or wind-down scenario. Practically, that means if any of these companies is sold or liquidated for less than the current carrying value, BTX recovers its investment first before common shareholders take losses. The preferred structure is how institutional private-equity investors almost always hold their positions, and it is a quiet NAV-protection mechanism that pure-common private holdings do not have. Against a book where approximately a third of NAV is private, this feature materially reduces the tail risk of a mark cycle. (It is important to note that while preferred stock sits above common equity, it remains junior to every tranche of debt, including senior secured, senior unsecured, subordinated notes, and convertible debt until converted, so creditors are made whole in a wind-down before any preferred holder recovers a dollar.)

Second, this type of exposure to these companies is very hard to replicate in terms of retail exposure. The ability to own these names, especially through a single CEF, is unusual. The addition of OpenAI in Q1 2026 at 2.59% of the portfolio is the most important new disclosure in the March 31 fact sheet. BlackRock has historically been able to access allocations in oversubscribed frontier AI rounds across its private markets platform (such as BlackRock being a significant investor in the Anthropic $183 billion Series F round or the $380 billion Series G round), and BTX shareholders are now getting fractional exposure to a round that is otherwise gated to institutional-only investors at valuations in the hundreds of billions of dollars range. Anthropic itself has been the subject of sustained funding-round markups across 2024 and 2025 as frontier model revenues have scaled, and BTX's Q1 2026 weight increase to 3.52% (from 2.69% in December) suggests either fresh capital into a new round or a fair-value markup on the existing position. Either reading is a positive signal.

Notable PE Holdings

PsiQuantum

This is the largest PE position and the fund's largest overall holding. PsiQuantum is building a photonic fault-tolerant quantum computer and is pre-revenue. Sovereign partnerships include the Australian government (A USD $620 million commitment for a quantum facility in Brisbane), the UK (STFC Daresbury and the National Quantum Computing Centre), and the US (Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park in Chicago). PsiQuantum raised $750 million in Series E in late 2025 at a reported $6 billion valuation, up from approximately $3.15 billion in 2021. The $65.7 million carry is supported by the late 2025 round and represents BTX's thesis on fault-tolerant quantum reaching commercial scale later this decade.

Anthropic

Anthropic is one of the main Frontier AI labs behind the Claude model family. Anthropic raised at an approx. $61 billion valuation in early 2025 and approx. $183 billion valuation in the October 2025 round, with press reports of further funding discussions at higher valuations in Q1 2026. Revenue run-rate scaled from approx. $1 billion at the start of 2025 to over $5 billion by August 2025, and Claude Code alone reached a $2.5 billion run-rate by early 2026.

Deepgram

Deepgram is a speech AI API specialist competing with OpenAI's Whisper, ElevenLabs, and AssemblyAI. Deepgram announced a $130 million Series C at a $1.3 billion valuation on January 13, 2026, with BlackRock participating again as an existing investor. Deepgram has strong enterprise traction with real revenue and a credible path to a premium mark at the next raise, with IBM partnering with Deepgram as their first voice partner. The $31.2 million holding likely understates fair value if the proposed 2026 round prices well.

OpenAI

OpenAI is another frontier AI lab, creator of the GPT family (such as ChatGPT). OpenAI now operates as OpenAI Group PBC following the 2025 corporate restructuring to a Public Benefit Corporation, 26%-owned by the nonprofit OpenAI Foundation. Funding has scaled from $157 billion post-money in October 2024 through a $300 billion SoftBank-led round in April 2025, a $500 billion secondary tender in October 2025, and most recently the $110 billion Amazon/Nvidia/SoftBank round that closed in February 2026 at $730 billion pre-money and then subsequently extended to $122 billion at an $852 billion valuation as of March 2026. This makes OpenAI the most valuable private technology company in history.

Databricks

Databricks is a data and AI platform, originally the Spark commercialization company. Now, Databricks is a unified analytics, data engineering, and ML platform. Databricks had a $5 billion Series L equity round, alongside $2 billion in new debt at a $134 billion valuation, completed in February 2026. Revenue scaled from approx. $3 billion ARR at Series J to $5.4 billion ARR with 65% YoY growth, with $1.4 billion in AI product revenue by Q4 2025. The company achieved positive free cash flow for the full year 2025. This company is the highest-profile IPO candidate in the book, in my opinion.

Patreon

Patreon is a creator-economy subscription platform valued at approximately $4 billion in the April 2021 Series F led by Tiger Global. Creator-economy growth has materially slowed after 2022, and the category faces live competition from Substack, Kajabi, Beehiiv, and direct-to-fan tooling built into social platforms. The $10.9 million BTX carry reflects multiple compressions. Periodic IPO talk has surfaced, but a liquidity event at or near the 2021 valuation looks unlikely.

Discord

Discord is a popular gaming and community communications platform. Discord filed confidentially for an IPO on January 6, 2026, with Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan as lead underwriters, targeting a Q1 2026 public debut. Humam Sakhnini became CEO in April 2025, replacing co-founder Jason Citron. Discord has real revenue and a massive user base, but monetization conversion has been a persistent challenge. The $7.7 million BTX carry sits beneath the 2021 cost and is marked to reflect the slower growth path. An IPO within the next two quarters at any valuation in the $6-15 billion range would be a good value for BTX's position.

Additional Discussion on Holdings

The frontier cluster at approx. 17% of NAV is the live underwriting and the reason to own the fund. Databricks at 2.2% is the cleanest near-term IPO exit. The preferred-stock structure across the book provides liquidation-preference protection at each position. An investor buying BTX today at a -17% market-price discount is paying approx. $0.83 per dollar of reported NAV for a private book where approx. half the weight is fresh underwriting at current market valuations and approx. half is legacy holdings.

However, the opacity concern remains real. Private equity marks are quarterly, model-dependent, and lagged. But the combination of preferred-stock structure, fresh vintages (Anthropic and Databricks entered Q3 2025 at prices that are close to current comps rather than stale 2021 legacy holdings), and BlackRock's private-markets infrastructure underwriting the valuations creates a mark-to-market risk profile that is less severe than a pure-play private fund with older vintages and common-stock exposure.

Distributions

The distribution story is the worst part of the setup. Looking at the Q4 2025 commentary again, BTX's estimated source of distributions paid during the current fiscal year to date is 100% return of capital as of December 2025, and the total estimated source of distributions since inception is also 100% return of capital. The fund has paid $4.19 per share cumulatively against an IPO price of $20, and every penny of that has been classified as ROC rather than net investment income or realized capital gains. An 8.51% distribution rate funded entirely by return of capital is not a yield in the traditional income sense. It is a scheduled withdrawal from shareholder capital, re-labeled, which from a tax standpoint does have the benefit of reducing cost basis rather than generating current taxable income either.

Term Trust Structure

BTX has a dissolution date of March 25, 2033. If the fund dissolves on schedule, each shareholder receives a pro rata portion of net assets, which means the current approx. -17.23% market price discount to NAV converges to zero (subject to whatever NAV is at dissolution). That is a 7-year catalyst for approx. 17% of price return pure from discount closure, on top of any NAV growth from the underlying portfolio.

However, the Board may vote to extend the Dissolution Date once for up to one year and once more for up to an additional six months, taking the latest dissolution out to September 2034. The Board may also cause the fund to conduct an Eligible Tender Offer within 12 months preceding the Dissolution Date at NAV. If the resulting fund size after the tender is at least $200 million, the board may vote (without shareholder approval) to eliminate the dissolution date entirely and convert BTX to perpetual existence.

The realistic 2032-2033 path is a tender at NAV for shareholders who want out, combined with a perpetual-fund conversion for the remainder. Shareholders who want the full discount closure get it by tendering. Shareholders who stay get a fund that keeps trading at whatever discount the market assigns.

Tradability

The fund runs, on average, approx. 2 million in volume daily. Bid-ask spreads on the listing generally are tight and are around 1-2 cents. This is strong liquidity for a CEF and supports retail trading. Additionally, BTX trades at a NAV discount of ~17.5%, which adds to the buy rating, based on what this article has previously discussed. Shares outstanding are approx. 116.7 million, putting market cap at approx. $863 million against fund AUM of $1.04 billion. AUM growth from $921 million in January 2026 to $1.04 billion in April 2026 is a positive signal on net fund inflows and NAV appreciation.

Downside Risks

A large part of the downside risk of BTX lies in the risks associated with alternative investments (such as private equity). The 32% private book is level 3, marked quarterly, and relies on inputs from secondary-market transactions, recent funding rounds, and model-based DCF. A sharp correction in public AI comps would feed through to private marks with a 1-2 quarter lag. In a pullback scenario, BTX holders would see NAV decline in subsequent quarters even after public tech stabilized.

Additionally, for longer-term investors, the Eligible Tender Offer mechanism, combined with the $200 million post-tender minimum and the board's ability to eliminate the dissolution date unilaterally, means investors cannot count on a hard NAV event in 2033. The discount closure path is probabilistic and not a certainty.

Takeaway

BTX is a Buy. The post-February 2025 portfolio is positioned for the AI infrastructure and frontier-model investment cycle, and Tony Kim's Q1 2026 rotation confirms the team is running the fund with conviction around the capex theme. The public book is built around the hyperscaler supply chain (NVIDIA, Lumentum, Tower Semiconductor, Fabrinet, Asia Vital Components, and Elite Material). The private book delivers the combination of retail-accessible frontier AI exposure that no ETF or mutual fund provides, with Anthropic (3.52%), OpenAI (2.59%), PsiQuantum (7.10%), and Deepgram (3.51%) in the top 10. Currently, BTX is an exciting opportunity to get good private equity exposure to ride the AI buildout at a good NAV discount as well.

data.title: Drop In U.K. Unemployment Isn't All It Seems
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

Is the UK jobs market at a turning point? It might be tempting to look at the relatively sharp drop in unemployment in the three months to February and conclude the answer is “yes."

The unemployment rate dropped from 5.2% to 4.9%. But crucially, this does not appear to be because of a big shift into work. Employment was little changed over the past quarter. Instead, the details reveal the drop in the jobless rate is pretty much solely down to a rise in “economic inactivity” - that is, people neither in work nor actively seeking it. And the Office for National Statistics notes that this was particularly visible for students. We’d be cautious about reading too much into this, but the bottom line is the drop in unemployment isn’t necessarily the “good news” story it first seems.

That’s further backed up by the more timely data on company payrolls (PAYE). Private sector employment continues to drop, driven primarily by consumer services. On a three-month annualised basis, employee numbers are falling by 1.6% in these sectors, which include hospitality and retail. And that pace of decline is showing no sign of abating.

That is not a backdrop that is conducive to a renewed spike in wage growth. Private sector pay growth dipped to 3.2% in the most recent data, which, according to the Bank of England’s analysis in February, is consistent with a 2% inflation target. With headline inflation poised to rise towards 4% in Q3, it suggests real wages are set to fall and pressure on economic growth will mount.

Overall, today’s report is a reminder that the UK jobs market is going into the current energy crisis in a fragile state - and crucially, much weaker than it was in February 2022 at the onset of the last oil/natural gas shock. For the time being, we think the Bank will opt against rate hikes this year, keeping the Bank Rate at 3.75% instead.
